Q1: I tried to keep things pretty steady throughout the semester, usually blocking out about three hours a day for coursework. Being a kinesthetic learner, I'd often study while doing something physical—like reviewing notes while pacing around my room or working through problems at a standing desk. I found that having a loose routine helped me stay on track, even when my motivation was low. Some weeks were better than others, but I tried to maintain consistency.
Q2: My pattern was fairly regular, honestly. I'd usually hit the books in the late afternoon after my extracurricular activities, since that's when I felt most ready to focus. Deadlines would sometimes push me to add extra hours, but I didn't like cramming—it just stressed me out more. I preferred spreading things out even if it meant slower progress overall.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or seeing if there were updates to the course materials. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if anyone had responded to my discussion posts, since I actually enjoyed those interactions with classmates. Notifications on my phone would remind me too, which helped since my motivation wasn't always there to push me on its own.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee and then again in the evening. It wasn't exciting or anything, but it became like checking email—just something you do. Having those peer interactions kept me coming back because I genuinely liked seeing what other people thought about the material. The routine helped me stay engaged even during the times I felt pretty down.
Q5: The discussion forums were honestly the most valuable for me because I learn better when I can talk things through with others. The interactive exercises were good too since I could actually do something rather than just read passively. Videos were okay, but I'd often watch them while doing something with my hands to stay focused. Anything that let me engage actively rather than just absorb information passively worked better for my learning style.
Q6: I'll admit I skipped a lot of the supplementary readings and some of the longer video lectures. When you're already feeling low on motivation, staring at walls of text just doesn't cut it. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came. The stuff that felt optional and didn't directly connect to assignments usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. If I could actually do something—work through a problem, build something, practice a skill—that felt valuable. I also appreciated activities where I could interact with classmates because that social element made the learning feel less isolating, especially during the times I was feeling sad.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford, especially with high stress levels and limited energy. Required ones got done because they had to get done—there was accountability. With optional stuff, it was easy to tell myself it could wait, and then it just never happened. The pressure of requirements actually helped me stay on track in a weird way.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and would work through them on paper, making notes and diagrams by hand. I'd also use physical textbooks and sometimes YouTube videos that explained concepts differently. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer—I'd be at my desk with printed materials spread out, working through problems manually. That hands-on approach worked better for me than clicking through screens.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper or building models to understand concepts, and none of that showed up anywhere in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides once and then spend days annotating physical copies. Sometimes I'd study with friends from my extracurriculars in person, working through material together, and obviously that collaboration was invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download videos and watch them on my tablet while at the gym or walking around campus. PDFs got printed and marked up extensively offline. I'd access something once to get it, then the real engagement happened completely disconnected from the internet. My actual study time was way more than what the LMS could track.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when the sadness hit harder and my motivation just tanked. I'd still log in and go through the motions, but it felt like I was underwater. The stress piled up even though I tried to manage it, and some weeks I was just surviving rather than thriving. My extracurriculars helped me cope, but they also took energy I didn't always have.
Q13: The high stress definitely impacted how I engaged with the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum—check what was due, submit what needed submitting, and log off. Fatigue from balancing everything made it hard to do the deeper engagement I knew I should be doing. Some days I'd stare at the screen and realize I'd absorbed nothing because my mind was elsewhere dealing with everything else going on.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or let videos play without really taking anything in. My body was present but my mind was checked out, usually because I was stressed or feeling low. I'd log the activity but couldn't tell you what I'd supposedly learned. It was like going through the motions without the substance.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts sometimes even though I knew they helped my learning, just because initiating felt like too much effort when I was down. Some of the bigger assignments got pushed closer to deadlines than they should have because starting them felt overwhelming. I knew it was important, but low motivation made everything feel heavier than it actually was. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but the delay was real.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation since my internal motivation wasn't reliable. I'd structure my week around what was due and when, working backward to make sure I had enough time. The LMS became more active for me in the days leading up to submissions—checking rubrics, reviewing materials, making sure I hadn't missed anything. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted more than I did.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though sometimes not in the way you'd expect. Good feedback on my assignments—like those high scores I got—gave me a boost and made me want to engage more. But waiting for feedback was stressful, and sometimes I'd avoid checking the LMS because I was anxious about what I'd find. The CBT sessions I did helped me manage those reactions better over time.
Q18: The course structure helped me stay organized since I could see what was coming and plan around it. Having regular discussion posts kept me connected to classmates, which was important for my mood. The pacing was mostly manageable, though some weeks felt heavier than others. I appreciated that there was flexibility in how I could engage with materials, even if I didn't always take advantage of it.
Q19: The interactive elements made engagement easier because they matched how I learn best. What made it harder was when there were long stretches of passive content—reading-heavy modules or lengthy video lectures without breaks. The discussion forums being active helped a lot because I could learn through conversation. More hands-on activities would have been even better for someone like me.
Q20: I think sometimes people are genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce output—maybe they're confused, overwhelmed, or dealing with stuff outside of class. They might be exploring, trying to understand, but not yet ready to commit to submitting work. High activity could also mean they're searching for help or clarification because they're stuck. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the system, so my LMS activity probably undersells my actual engagement. Someone could download materials once and study them for hours—the system only sees the download. Performance comes from understanding, and understanding can happen in lots of ways that aren't tracked.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, submission timing. But they miss the quality of engagement completely. They can't tell if I was actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline study, emotional state, whether the material actually clicked. The numbers tell a story, but it's only a partial one.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to things I already know. For me, it also involves interacting with others—discussing, debating, explaining. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time logged on a platform. True engagement feels active, not passive.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with motivation and sadness throughout the course, or how much effort it took just to maintain steady performance. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the peer support from my extracurricular friends, or the work I did in CBT to manage my stress. My resilience kept me going, but that inner battle is completely invisible in the data. The numbers say I passed steadily, but they don't show what it cost me to get there.
